name: fd-dual-database-state-divergence
focus: State consistency between SQLite (Node host) and Postgres (Rust daemon) across all entity types
persona: A distributed systems engineer who specializes in dual-write patterns, eventual consistency, and the failure modes that emerge when two independent databases hold overlapping state. Approaches every write path asking: what happens if only one side succeeds?
decision_lens: Prioritizes findings where silent divergence causes behavioral differences — especially where one database is the source of truth for decisions (e.g., routing, trigger matching) but writes to it are not confirmed. Escalates any path where container_config, containerConfig, or requiresTrigger can be set in SQLite but not propagated to Postgres or vice versa.
review_areas: Trace the register_group IPC flow end-to-end: Node calls setRegisteredGroup (SQLite only via db.ts), does it also call the intercomd /v1/db/registered-groups endpoint or writeToOutbox? Confirm whether container_config reaches Postgres.

Audit every callsite of setRegisteredGroup in db.ts — check whether the Postgres dual-write path is invoked for group registration, and whether containerConfig/runtime/model columns are included in the dual-write payload.

Check the outbox payload schema: writeToOutbox only handles 'message' and 'chat_metadata' payload types. Identify all entity types written to SQLite but with no outbox path and assess whether Rust reads authoritative data from Postgres or falls back to Node.

Examine the Telegram ingress path in intercomd: when Rust handles /v1/telegram/ingress, it reads registered groups from its own Postgres pool. Verify whether that Postgres table is populated for groups registered via Node IPC.
Inspect the fallback path when Postgres is unavailable: dualWriteToPostgres fires fire-and-forget HTTP. Confirm these endpoints exist and are wired in main.rs.
Check for schema drift: SQLite uses ALTER TABLE migrations; Postgres uses separate DDL. Confirm both schemas are in sync for container_config, context_mode, runtime, model columns.
success_hints: Every entity write path has an outbox entry or documented reason why Postgres does not need it.
container_config is confirmed to reach Postgres for all registration paths.
The Rust daemon's registered_groups Postgres table is populated at startup or on group registration.
task_context: Intercom is a dual-process system: a Node host handles WhatsApp/Telegram channels and writes to SQLite, while the Rust daemon (intercomd) handles orchestration and reads/writes Postgres. Known bugs include container_config only being stored in one database and the mount allowlist never being loaded in certain code paths.
anti_overlap: fd-queue-delivery-reliability covers how pending messages are drained from the queue after database writes succeed.
fd-container-lifecycle-management covers what happens to a running container when group configuration is stale.
fd-mount-security-wiring covers the allowlist load path and validation logic.

name: fd-queue-delivery-reliability
focus: End-to-end message delivery from inbound receipt through outbox drain to GroupQueue dispatch, with emphasis on pending-message drain failures
persona: A reliability engineer who traces message flows through async systems and looks for places where a message can be acknowledged but never processed, or where a retry loop can starve or permanently drop work. Reads code with queuing theory intuitions: what is the source of truth for 'pending', and who clears it?
decision_lens: Prioritizes paths where pendingMessages=true is set but the drain that clears it is never triggered, or where the outbox drain calls queue.enqueue_message_check but the group has no registered process_messages_fn yet. Treats any silent drop after max retries as high-severity.
review_areas: Audit the Rust retry path in queue.rs run_for_group: on failure, the retry spawns an async task that sets state.pending_messages=true after a delay — confirm whether this deferred flag will ever be picked up without a new inbound message.
Check the Node GroupQueue retry path: after MAX_RETRIES, retryCount resets and function returns. Verify whether a group can get stuck with no further retry trigger.
Trace the outbox drain signal path: LISTEN/NOTIFY to drain_tx to enqueue_message_check. Verify process_messages_fn is wired before the outbox drain starts in main.rs startup ordering.
Examine drain_pending (queue.rs): after reset_group, does it correctly iterate all groups with pending work? Check for concurrency limit enforcement mid-loop.
Check the DRAIN_FALLBACK_INTERVAL vs STALE_RECOVERY_INTERVAL timing: confirm 30-second fallback polling actually fires drain cycles that call enqueue_message_check.
Inspect Node GroupQueue shutdown behavior: shuttingDown=true prevents new enqueues, but pending retries (setTimeout) may fire after shutdown. Confirm callbacks guard against shuttingDown.
success_hints: Every pending_messages=true transition has a guaranteed corresponding drain trigger.
After max-retry exhaustion, there is a defined recovery path that does not require a new inbound message.
process_messages_fn is confirmed wired before the outbox drain loop starts.
task_context: Intercom is a dual-process system with a known bug where the queue does not drain pending messages in certain failure scenarios after container completion.
anti_overlap: fd-dual-database-state-divergence covers whether message rows reach Postgres at all.
fd-container-lifecycle-management covers container spawn failures and cleanup.
fd-mount-security-wiring covers allowlist loading and mount validation.

name: fd-container-lifecycle-management
focus: Container spawn, process registration, idle/timeout handling, and cleanup across Node and Rust execution paths
persona: A systems programmer who tracks process state machines and looks for places where a container is spawned but the parent loses track of it, or where cleanup is assumed but never confirmed.
decision_lens: Prioritizes findings where a container exits but active=true remains in the GroupQueue (preventing new spawns), or where a timeout fires after results but kills anyway. Escalates any path where a container holding a lock exits without releasing it.
review_areas: Audit the onProcess callback in container-runner.ts: verify registerProcess is always called before the container can exit. If it crashes immediately after spawn, does GroupQueue still reset active=false?
Check the Node timeout path: if stopContainer fails and SIGKILL is sent, confirm the finally block always executes and decrements activeCount.
Inspect Rust GroupQueue.reset_group: verify it is called in all terminal paths of run_for_group and run_task — specifically in panic/error cases and retry-schedule paths.
Check drain_pending (queue.rs): it collects candidates then checks active_count. If multiple groups become runnable and max_concurrent is reached mid-loop, confirm no over-spawn past the limit.
Examine container timeout defaults: if container_config is absent (known bug), what does the timeout fall back to? Is the fallback safe?

Check kill_group implementation: confirm container_name is set atomically with active=true — if a race allows kill_group before register_process, it finds no container_name and returns false silently.
success_hints: active=true / active_count++ always has corresponding active=false / active_count-- in every terminal path.
Container names are registered before any code path can call kill_group.
Container timeout is safe even when containerConfig is absent from both databases.
task_context: Intercom's dual-process architecture spawns containers from the Rust daemon. Known bugs include container_config only set in one database, affecting timeout values.
anti_overlap: fd-dual-database-state-divergence covers whether containerConfig reaches both databases.
fd-queue-delivery-reliability covers message dispatch into the queue.
fd-mount-security-wiring covers mount validation before container argument construction.

name: fd-mount-security-wiring
focus: End-to-end wiring of the mount allowlist — from load at startup through validation to container argument construction
persona: A security engineer who audits filesystem isolation boundaries in containerized systems. Traces every code path from additionalMounts to the final docker run -v argument, asking whether the allowlist is always consulted.
decision_lens: Prioritizes findings where the allowlist is optional but its absence silently allows or blocks mounts without alerting the operator, where symlink resolution can be gamed, and where Node-side and Rust-side implementations diverge in security rules.
review_areas: Trace the allowlist load path in Node (mount-security.ts): loadMountAllowlist() uses module-level cache. If file does not exist at start, all mounts are blocked forever. Verify whether MOUNT_ALLOWLIST_PATH resolves correctly in systemd service context.
Compare Node vs Rust allowlist load paths: confirm both use the same file path. Does Rust load at spawn time or daemon startup?
Audit blocked-pattern matching in both implementations: Node uses substring match (part.includes(pattern)). Confirm this is intentional and does not over-block legitimate paths.
Check containerPath validation: both reject '..' and absolute paths. If containerPath defaults to basename(hostPath), verify basename cannot be empty or a dotfile bypassing blocked-pattern checks.
Examine the exclude/tmpfs overlay mechanism: for excluded subdirs, verify values are validated against path traversal before being passed as tmpfs destination arguments.
Verify Rust runner invokes validate_additional_mounts before building docker args, and that Postgres containerConfig flows from group registration through to the runner.
success_hints: Allowlist file path resolves correctly under the systemd service user's HOME.
Exclude subdir values in additionalMounts are validated for path traversal.
Node and Rust mount validation apply the same rules in the same order.
task_context: A known bug is that the mount allowlist was never loaded in main.rs (hardcoded to None), causing all additional mounts to be silently blocked.
anti_overlap: fd-dual-database-state-divergence covers whether containerConfig reaches both databases.
fd-container-lifecycle-management covers container spawn after mount args are built.

name: fd-ipc-authorization-boundary
focus: IPC authorization model: whether group identity derived from filesystem path is enforced consistently across Node and Rust, and whether cross-group privilege escalation is possible
persona: A security researcher who specializes in confused deputy attacks and authorization boundary bypasses in filesystem-based IPC systems. Asks: what proves identity, and can that proof be forged?
decision_lens: Prioritizes findings where Rust and Node apply different authorization rules for the same IPC operation, where sourceGroup identity can be spoofed, and where main-group privileges can be exercised by a non-main group.
review_areas: Audit Node IPC watcher identity model (ipc.ts): sourceGroup is derived from the directory name. Verify no code path mounts the entire DATA_DIR/ipc/ directory, which would let a container write to other groups' subdirectories.
Check register_group IPC handler: only main can register. But the handler writes to SQLite without writing to Postgres. Confirm whether this creates a SQLite-only group invisible to the Rust daemon.
Examine whether intercomd has its own IPC file poller or relies on Node host-callback. If Rust polls IPC directly, confirm it applies the same sourceGroup identity checks.
Inspect host-callback /v1/ipc/forward-task: Rust passes is_main as a boolean. Verify intercomd derives is_main from its own group registry, not from a container-influenced field.
Check cross-group message authorization (ipc.ts): non-main can only message their own group. Verify registeredGroups() is called fresh, not from stale cache.
Audit schedule_task authorization: non-main can only schedule for themselves. Confirm folder uniqueness is enforced at registration time to prevent cross-group scheduling via folder collision.
success_hints: IPC directory mount per group is confirmed — no container can write to another group's IPC namespace.
is_main is derived from Rust's own registry, never from container IPC payload.
Group folder uniqueness enforced by UNIQUE constraint in both databases.
task_context: Boundary-crossing failures have been found at IPC, database, and mount layers. This agent focuses on whether authorization rules at the IPC layer are applied consistently by both processes.
anti_overlap: fd-dual-database-state-divergence covers whether registrations reach both databases.
fd-mount-security-wiring covers filesystem mount isolation underlying IPC namespace separation.
fd-queue-delivery-reliability covers message dispatch after authorization passes.
